content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 27, 1992

Peter Leigh Snell
Public Affairs Support Services
United Parcel Service
401 Wythe Street, Suite 2-A
Alexandria, Virginia 22314

RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-92-131

Dear Mr. Snell:

You have requested advice on behalf of the United Parcel
Service Public Affairs Support Services, its political action
committee, United Parcel Service Political Action Committee, and
the United Parcel Service Political Action Committee-California
concerning the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act"). 1/

Your letter of February 25, 1992, correctly summarizes the
advice provided to you by the staff of the Commission's Technical
Assistance and Analysis Division except for the reporting
procedures outlined in number 3. and number 7.

Number 3. provides that UPSPAC-California will be funded by
receiving an allocated portion of certain individual contributions
to the UPSPAC's separate segregated fund. The committee's
contributions are received through solicitations of UPS
stockholders, executive and administrative personnel. Such
solicitations make clear that contributions to the UPSPAC are used
to support state and local as well as federal campaigns.

Persons who do not receive contributions but who make
contributions aggregating $10,000 or more in a calendar year to
support California state and local candidates and committees are
required to file campaign statements. (Section 82013.) It is
therefore recommended that a statement be included in the

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Divisions 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Peter Leigh Snell
Page 2


committee's fundraising solicitations instructing persons to
contact UPSPAC to determine how much of their contribution went to
the UPSPAC-California committee if those persons are making other
contributions outside UPSPAC to California state and local
candidates and committees.

Number 7. provides that for purposes of compliance with the
reporting of non-monetary contributions received by the UPSPAC-
California, UPS proposes to attribute a percentage of certain
total UPSPAC solicitation costs as non-monetary contributions by
UPS to the UPSPAC-California.

The Act provides that whenever a committee receives goods,
services, facilities, or anything of value other than money, it is
required to report the fair market value of the donated items.
Generally, the fair market value is determined by whatever it
would cost the committee to purchase the goods or services.
(Section 82025.5.) If your example of attributing a percentage of
the solicitation costs accurately represents the fair market value
of what it would cost UPSPAC-California to raise the $40,000
allocated to the committee, the allocation method outlined in your
example is an acceptable means for determining the fair market
value of the non-monetary contribution.

If you have any additional questions concerning this matter,
please do not hesitate to contact me at 916/322-5662.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

[signature]

By: Mary Ann Kvasager
Political Reform Consultant

sections.question_synthetic: What are the campaign reporting requirements and procedures for the United Parcel Service Political Action Committee-California regarding contributions, solicitations, and non-monetary contributions under California's Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that contributors making $10,000 or more annually to California state and local candidates must file campaign statements, fundraising solicitations should instruct persons to contact UPSPAC to determine contribution allocations, and attributing a percentage of solicitation costs as non-monetary contributions is acceptable if it accurately represents fair market value.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document lacks formal QUESTION and CONCLUSION section headers; extracted question and conclusion from body text discussing advice on reporting procedures and non-monetary contribution valuation. Technical nature of campaign finance regulations extracted from discussion with requestor.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What are the campaign reporting requirements and procedures for the United Parcel Service Political Action Committee-California regarding contributions, solicitations, and non-monetary contributions under California's Political Reform Act?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC advises that contributors making $10,000 or more annually to California state and local candidates must file campaign statements, fundraising solicitations should instruct persons to contact UPSPAC to determine contribution allocations, and attributing a percentage of solicitation costs as non-monetary contributions is acceptable if it accurately represents fair market value.

Number 3. provides that UPSPAC-California will be funded by receiving an allocated portion of certain individual contributions to the UPSPAC's separate segregated fund. The committee's contributions are received through solicitations of UPS stockholders, executive and administrative personnel. Such solicitations make clear that contributions to the UPSPAC are used to support state and local as well as federal campaigns. Persons who do not receive contributions but who make contributions aggregating $10,000 or more in a calendar year to support California state and local candidates and committees are required to file campaign statements. (Section 82013.) It is therefore recommended that a statement be included in the 1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Divisions 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 Peter Leigh Snell Page 2 committee's fundraising solicitations instructing persons to contact UPSPAC to determine how much of their contribution went to the UPSPAC-California committee if those persons are making other contributions outside UPSPAC to California state and local candidates and committees. Number 7. provides that for purposes of compliance with the reporting of non-monetary contributions received by the UPSPAC- California, UPS proposes to attribute a percentage of certain total UPSPAC solicitation costs as non-monetary contributions by UPS to the UPSPAC-California. The Act provides that whenever a committee receives goods, services, facilities, or anything of value other than money, it is required to report the fair market value of the donated items. Generally, the fair market value is determined by whatever it would cost the committee to purchase the goods or services. (Section 82025.5.) If your example of attributing a percentage of the solicitation costs accurately represents the fair market value of what it would cost UPSPAC-California to raise the $40,000 allocated to the committee, the allocation method outlined in your example is an acceptable means for determining the fair market value of the non-monetary contribution. If you have any additional questions concerning this matter, please do not hesitate to contact me at 916/322-5662. Sincerely, Scott Hallabrin Acting General Counsel [signature] By: Mary Ann Kvasager Political
embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter to United Parcel Service clarifying campaign reporting procedures under the Political Reform Act, specifically regarding contribution thresholds, solicitation disclosures, and valuation of non-monetary contributions attributed to UPSPAC-California.
